nativeRange.habitat: Seaward reef slopes, typically at depths of 6–80 m; adults tend to favor steep outer reef slopes deeper than 25 m, in areas rich with gorgonians and black corals.

diet.notes: Feeds on small invertebrates including snails, worms, mussels, small crustaceans, and small fish. Juveniles are known to act as cleaner fish, picking parasites off other species. Adults tend to hunt by blowing water over sandy substrate to uncover hidden prey. Generally accepts frozen meaty foods such as krill, mysis, and chopped shellfish.

summary: A striking Indo-Pacific wrasse with a reddish-orange face and white posterior, known for its lyre-shaped tail. Juveniles act as cleaner fish; adults tend to be semi-aggressive and will consume most invertebrates.
careNotes:
The Lyretail Hogfish is generally considered an intermediate-level species. Juveniles are peaceful and have been observed removing parasites from tankmates, making them a useful early addition. As they mature, they tend to become semi-aggressive and should be housed with similarly robust, moderately aggressive tankmates. They have been known to target small fish, ornamental shrimp, snails, small clams, and worms — so are commonly considered incompatible with reef invertebrates. Isolated reports suggest they ignore corals directly, but their hunting of reef invertebrates generally earns them a 'with caution' reef designation.

This species revels in swimming and generally benefits from an aquarium with ample open water column space as well as rocky hiding places. Their sand-sifting hunting behavior can temporarily cloud the water and stir up detritus. A tight-fitting lid is advised; multiple sources note this species has been known to jump from open-top aquariums.

Temperature disagreement: LiveAquaria lists 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), FreshMarine lists 75–82°F (23.9–27.8°C), and FishBase records wild habitat temperatures of 24.4–28.9°C. Averaged across sources: approximately 23–27°C.

RESOLVED 2026-06-03: Tank size disagreement investigated with two additional sources. ReefApp confirms 600 L; reeflex.net recommends 1300 L; a broader hobbyist consensus (UltimateReef, Aquatics Unlimited) references 100 gal (~380 L) as a practical minimum for adults. The original 300 L figure was at the extreme low end. Updated to 400 L — aligning with the ~100 gal community consensus and the nice-values list — as a defensible minimum for a juvenile that will reach 24 cm. Upgraded aquariums are still advisable for large adults. Also corrected escapeRisk from 'low' to 'medium' based on documented jumping behavior. Sex-change field (protogynous) confirmed correct by ReefApp: this species changes from female to male, consistent with the protogynous pattern common to Labridae.
breedingNotes: Reported to be oviparous with distinct pairing during breeding. As a member of the Labridae family, this species is likely protogynous. Breeding in captivity has not been well documented.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Product page with care sheet. Listed minimum tank as 70 gal, temperature 72–78°F, SG 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (24.0 cm SL), native range, taxonomy (family Labridae, order Eupercaria), and habitat/depth. FishBase temperature range 24.4–28.9°C differs from LiveAquaria; averaged across sources.

additional.name: ReefApp
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (24 cm), reef safe status ('with caution'), and tank volume recommendation (600 L). Tank size is substantially larger than LiveAquaria's 70 gal / 265 L; this wide spread triggered needs_review.

additional.name: FreshMarine
additional.notes: Additional cross-check: adult size up to 9 in (22.9 cm), minimum 75 gal, temperature 75–82°F (23.9–27.8°C), SG 1.020–1.025, care level moderate, semi-aggressive, reef safe with caution.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeding in home aquariums has not been reliably documented. Reported to be oviparous with distinct pairing behavior in the wild.
